other_observers: none; found and identified by me
description: the bird was a small flycatcher, much smaller than a kingbird, but somewhat larger than a Least Flycatcher I saw just before. It was identified as a Wood-Pewee by a combination of a very indistinct eye-ring, very long (compared to a an Empid) primary projection, by the dusky colored chest, the noticeably crested look, and dusky, indistinct wingbars. I did not see the bird well enough to notice whether or not the physical features favored one wood-pewee species or the other, only well enough to tell that it was a wood-pewee.Behaviors: The bird was singing high in a tree, where it was hard to see. After it stopped singing, it moved off to where I could not see it. I heard the bird sing about a half-dozen times or so.Call: The song, which was heard well a half-dozen times or so, was a a falling then strongly rising "pee-a-wee!", without the burry quality associated with Western Wood-Pewee song.Plumage: alternate
